title: Quantitative Reasoning, Estimation, and Problem Framing · Guided Modeling Studio
summary: Estimate attendance, supplies, time, and uncertainty for a neighborhood event before exact values are available. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of a transparent event-planning estimate notebook while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Interpret a number together with its unit, reference population, time window, and practical meaning.
Estimate magnitudes with benchmarks and defend an appropriate tolerance before calculating exactly.
Translate a practical question into known quantities, unknown quantities, constraints, and operations.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.python:
"""applied-mathematics-beginner cumulative model portfolio."""

from math import exp, log, sqrt


def reasonableness(value: float, lower: float, upper: float) -> bool:
    return lower <= value <= upper

# Add one tested model increment per activity.

options.text: Define the stakeholder decision, quantities, authority, and constraints
Choose and document the smallest defensible model or procedure
Execute the model while preserving inspectable intermediate evidence
Test boundaries and changed cases, communicate limits, and revise

steps.title: Produce a checked model result
steps.instruction: A measured quantity starts at 20 units, changes by −7 units, then by +4 units. Calculate the final quantity.
steps.why: A numerical result is not complete until its unit, assumptions, scale, and tolerance survive a reasonableness check.

content.text: Preserve the signs and compute 20 − 7 + 4; compare with the starting scale.

content.title: Before calculating
content.text: Predict the sign and approximate magnitude. Enter the requested quantity with a unit, then compare it with that prediction.

hints: Preserve the signs and compute 20 − 7 + 4; compare with the starting scale.
Keep full working precision and round only the final reported value.
The accepted result is within 0.001 of the exact value and includes units.

steps.title: Encode a reproducible check
steps.instruction: Add a evidence_math_beg_assumption_record_1aoizp function that computes or traces the changed case, asserts one meaningful invariant, and returns inspectable evidence.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Assumption Record into observable performance evidence.

content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The structural check is only one gate. The following changed-case decision and explanation must also hold.

stimulus.title: Assumption Record failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: Assumptions should remain hidden so an answer looks certain.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: State assumptions and distinguish measured, given, estimated, and derived values.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Assumption Record condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model
steps.instruction: Write a concise evidence note that explains when Assumption Record applies, what can fail, and how the changed case was verified.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: State assumptions and distinguish measured, given, estimated, and derived values.
